title: Patient sex Differences and MMPI Changes—1958 to 1969
abstract:
185
Patient Sex Differences and
MMPI Changes-1958 to 1969
David G. Rice, PhD, and
Joseph G. Kepecs, MD, Madison, Wis
THE PAST decade has been a period of
rapid social change. The effects of this are
perhaps no more evident than in the areas of
sexual and social role expectations and be-
havior. There is much evidence (visual and
graphic)
that we may be moving in an
androgynous direction-toward a "unisex."
This would seem to have many psychiatric
implications.1-4 In discussing these trends,
Winick5 concludes:
Multivalent,
amorphous,
and
depolarized
roles might theoretically lead to increased flexi-
bility and options in behavior, but in actuality
may tend to invoke uncertainty. Such tolerance
of ambiguity is desirable for a healthy per-
sonality, but today's environment and culture
are ambiguous enough to tax the adaptability
of even the healthiest personalities (p 24).
We attempted
to empirically document
the psychological byproducts of these socie-
tal changes by studying the Minnesota Mul-
tiphasic
Personality
Inventory
(MMPI)
scale scores of patients during two time peri-
ods-1958 to 1960 and 1967 to 1969. It was
assumed that younger psychiatric patients
would
be
especially
affected by
rapidly
changing sexual and social role expectations,
and that the MMPI data of two carefully
matched groups from the above time periods
would reflect this. We were especially inter-
ested in ascertaining whether male and fe-
male patients respond differentially to the

pressures of shifting social and sexual role
expectations.
There is a rather surprising paucity of
studies
using
the
MMPI
to
document
changes in matched groups of individuals
over time. Most MMPI investigators con-
cerned with change have attempted serial
testing of a given individual over relatively
short periods of time,6-9 eg, during a period
of hospitalization. Such studies have docu-
mented an adequate degree of reliability of
the MMPI for repeated measurements. The
ability of the test to discern differences be-
tween matched groups over a lengthy time
period has surprisingly rarely been studied
empirically.10 This was the focus in the pres-
ent study.
Method
Test batteries of all patients 30 years of age
or under referred for psychological testing in a
large university medical center during the peri-
od January 1958 to June 1969 were examined.
Distributions of patients across years according
to age, sex, and educational level were per-
formed.
The goal for the present study was to select
two matches samples of adequate statistical size
made up of patients reflecting as great a time
separation as was feasible from our testing
records. In accordance with this goal, all men
and women (N = 100) referred for testing and
given at least a Rorschach and an MMPI
during 1958 to 1960 were included and desig-
nated as earlier groups EM and EF. Each
subject in the EM and EF groups was carefully
matched with a subject tested during 1967 to

total patient population at the medical center
during the later time period sampled. In es-
sence, we forced the later sample to conform
roughly to the demographic characteristics of
the earlier sample, in regard to proportion of
subjects in the age, sex, and educational attain-
ment classifications. To ascertain, for example,
whether the LF sample was representative, we
selected at random another sample of 40 female
patients from the total 1967 to 1969 population
and compared
the
ordering
of
scales
from
high-to-low on the mean MMPI profiles. A
rank order correlation of 0.997 (P < 0.001) for
the two samples suggested that our matched LF
sample was representative of the population,
despite the restrictions imposed by the match-
ing procedure. It is our belief that the earlier
and later samples are comprised of individuals
who adequately reflect the type and range of
patients 30 years of age and under who are
being seen or who were formerly seen in gen-
eral psychiatric inpatient and outpatient prac-
tice.
For the men, mean age was 21.76 years
(a = 4.07 years); two (4%)
patients had less
than nine years of education, 17 (30%) patients
had 9 to 12 years, and 37 (66%) patients 12+
years. In the EM sample, 33 (59%) were inpa-
tients at the time of testing and 23 (41%) were
outpatients. The LM sample was composed of
34

inpatients and 22
(39%)
outpa-
tients. For the women, mean age was 20.56 years
(a = 4.09); two (5%) of the patients had less
than nine years of education, 24 (54%) had 9

ations reflect strong positive skewing on this scale.
to 12 years, and 18 (41%) had 12+ years. In
the EF sample, 33 (75%) were inpatients at the
time of testing and 11 (25%) were outpatients.
The LF sample was composed of 35 (80%) in-
patients and nine (20%) outpatients.
There were three significant differences in
sample composition between male (EM and
LM) and female (EF and LF) groups in the
study:
(1) The men were older than the wom-
en, t = 2.07, df = 198 (P < 0.05). (2) More men
than women had some college, x2 = 5.35, df =
1 (P < 0.05).
(3) There were relatively more
male than female outpatients, x2 = 6.07, df = 1
(P < 0.02). Within each of the male and female
samples
(the main set of data for empirical
analysis), however, due to the matching pro-
cedure there were no
significant
differences
between early and late groups on age,
sex,
education level, or hospital status (inpatient vs
outpatient). As previously stated, the 1958 to
1960 population characteristics determined the
makeup of the overall male and female sam-
ples. Thus, one could conclude that during the
period 1958 to 1960:
(a) women seen by hospi-
tal psychiatrists, who made the testing refer-
rals, tended to be younger than the men seen;
(b) the women seen tended to have less educa-
tion; and (c) there were proportionately more
women than men seen on an inpatient basis.
These facts may not have been true for pa-
tients seen between 1967 and 1969, but they do
represent the case for the 1958 to 1960 sample,
and might be interpreted that women in our
society more quickly define and admit to prob-
lems and seek help earlier and with less social
censure than men.11

The responses on MMPI answer sheets for
the 200 subjects were then recoded on IBM
punch cards. This formed the data set for all
subsequent analyses in the study.

Additional MMPI Scale Analyses.-We
decided to investigate further sex differences
in MMPI change over time by looking at
some of the additional clinical and research
scales12,14 that we arbitrarily and subjective-
ly felt would show sensitivity to change over
time. Many of these were the subcomponent
scales proposed by Harris and Lingoes12 to
further "break down" several of the tradi-
tional clinical scales. A list of these scales
and means for the four groups is given in
significant EF-LF than EM-LM differences.

MMPI Item Analysis.-A more "micro-
scopic" inspection of the data was performed
the use of a computerized item
analysis. On each of the MMPI questions
the number of patients in each of the four
groups who answered "true" was compared
with the number who answered "false." The
x2 analyses indicated significant differences
between earlier and later groups of the same
sex on 33 of the 566 items for the women and
39 of the 566 items for the men. These items,
including direction of change and magnitude
of x2, are presented in Table 3. A subjective
attempt is made in Table 3 to group signif-
icant items by category. It is interesting to
note that although more individual items
were significant for men than women, the re-
sponses of the men, in contrast to those for
change on many of the patterns and clusters
that make up the traditional scales used in
One could conclude from the above data
shown more MMPI reflected change than
men during the past decade and (b) that
the change has been generally in the direc-
tion of an increase in MMPI psychopatho-
haps the most telling issue in regard to these
is whether female patients are
actually "sicker" now or whether they are
simply marking (ie, admitting to) "sicker"
items on the MMPI. A careful set of analy-
Wienerl' proposed a subtle-obvious break-
down of items on five of the ten MMPI
clinical scales (D, Hy, Pd, Pa, and Ma). If
is true that women are marking more
Arch Gen Psychiat-Vol 23, Aug 1970
"set" accounts for the sex differences found
in the study, then the later female groups
ought to be marking a significantly greater
than the earlier female groups. To test this,
repeated measures analyses of variance for
the four groups in the study on five scales
keyed to a subtle-obvious breakdown were
performed. In each analysis, only the "peri-
ods" term reached or tended toward signif-
icance: D, F = 11.54, P < 0.005; Hy, F =
5.53, P < 0.025; Pd, F = 3.81, P < 0.10, Pa,
F = 13.76, P < 0.005; and Ma, F = 6.62,
P < 0.025 (df = 1, 98 in each case). Neither
the "between sex" main effect or the "sex by
periods" interaction was significant for any
of the five scales. Thus, patients in the later
groups do tend to mark a higher proportion
of obvious items, as compared to patients in
the earlier groups. But this holds for both
men and women, and does not explain the
sex differences in MMPI change found in
A further test of the above proposition
was performed in terms of the acquiescence
response-set,17,18 or the general test-taking
tendency to say "true" to any item about
oneself. A repeated measures analysis of var-
iance on the number of "true" answers for
patients in each of the four groups indicated
similar results to the obvious-subtle analy-
ses. Only the "periods" terms tended toward
significance (F = 3.72, df, 1, 98, P < 0.10).
Both men and women in the later groups
give an increased number of "true" answers.
Means and standard deviations for the four
47.85; and LM, x = 260.25, a = 42.19. Thus,
one cannot explain the MMPI changes for
the women solely on the basis of an increase
in the test-taking tendency to mark as "true"
A final evaluation of the criticism that not
degree of female disturbance but only the
quality of female test-taking behavior has
changed was carried out by scoring the So-r
of items having socially "acceptable" conno-
means and standard deviations were: EF, x
EM, x = 26.05, r= 8.08; and LM, x = 24.82,
a= 6.71. A repeated measures analysis of
variance indicated a significant "sex" main
effect (F = 4.27, df, 1, 98, P < 0.05) and a
"periods" main effect which tended toward
significance (F = 3.85, df, 1, 98, P < 0.10).
Thus, women overall marked fewer So-r an-
swers than men, and both men and women in
the later groups marked fewer So-r items
than did patients in the earlier groups. Thus,
changes found as due solely to the tendency
for women to be admitting less "desirable"
things about themselves. Male patients in
the later groups also show a change in this
test taking "set" but, in contrast to the fe-
male patients, do not show the accompany-
ing magnitude of MMPI scale changes.
We conclude from the above data that
female patients under 30 show MMPI indi-
cations of an increase in psychopathology
during the past decade. This does not hold
It is our belief that these results are the
psychological by-products of the rapid social
change taking place during this time period.
It would appear that many, but not all, of
the significant differences obtained in the
study reflect a differential shift and change
in social and sexual role expectations. The
impact of this, as manifested on the MMPI,
is much greater for women than for men.
One way of understanding these results
would be in terms of society's "liberation" of
younger women. Thus, as it becomes socially
permissible for women to express impulses,
potential ones) who traditionally have dif-
ficulty with impulse expression are likely to
have greater difficulty. The majority of the
MMPI clinical scales showing higher eleva-
tion for the LF group are typical of "intro-
versive" patients (scales D, Pt, Sc, and Si),
who "hold feelings in" and are made anx-
ious by closeness to others with its potential
One would expect the ambiguity of pre-
scribed behavior generated during a period
of role expectancy change (a) to have rela-
tively more psychological impact the greater
it is at variance with previous, traditional
expectations, and (b) to effect more acutely
those individuals who have a general dif-
ficulty with social behavior. The changes in
Arch Gen Psychiat-Vol 23, Aug 1970
sexual and social role expectations during the
past decade seem to be greater and somewhat
more ambiguous for women than for men.
We believe this to account, in part, for the
significant sex differences found in the study.
The issue might be raised as to whether
amount of education was differentially re-
sponsible for the results obtained. To answer
this, the male and female samples were di-
vided into educational subgroups of 9 to 12
years and 12 + groups. Then standard MMPI
scales) were plotted for men and women in
these two educational groups. Visual inspec-
tion indicated that it was very unlikely that
standard MMPI scales could be accounted
for primarily on the basis of the patient's
education level. The relatively small number
of subjects in the educational samples, espe-
cially for the women (9 to 12 years, N = 24;
12 + years, N = 18) precluded careful empir-
ical analysis of this variable. In
both educational groups in the LF sample
had higher scale scores than patients in the
attempt a more thorough investigation of
this variable using larger samples composed
of patients with differing education levels.
The present study demonstrates that psy-
chological tests such as the MMPI can be
sensitive to changes in group behavior over
time. These changes are likely to mirror the
place during a given time period. In future
studies we intend to investigate other psy-
chological measures in this regard and to see
if the present findings can be
using a nonpatient sample and with groups
representing different age and educational
A group of 100 psychiatric patients under
30 years of age tested between 1958 and
1960 were compared on MMPI responses
with a carefully matched group tested dur-
ing 1967 to 1969. Marked sex differences
were obtained. Women in the later group
had many significantly higher mean clinical
and supplemental MMPI scale scores than
women in the earlier group. Earlier and later
male groups showed few significant scale
differences. Statistical analyses indicated that
these findings could not be explained in terms
of a differential response "set" for men and
women. The results suggest an increase in
MMPI indicated psychopathology for young-
er women, and were discussed in terms of the
rapidly changing sexual and social role ex-
pectations for women during the past decade.
